bibliographic_data.title: Multi-Camera Lighting Estimation for Photorealistic Front-Facing Mobile Augmented Reality
bibliographic_data.abstract: Lighting understanding plays an important role in virtual object composition, including mobile augmented reality (AR) applications. Prior work often targets recovering lighting from the physical environment to support photorealistic AR rendering. Because the common workflow is to use a back-facing camera to capture the physical world for overlaying virtual objects, we refer to this usage pattern as back-facing AR. However, existing methods often fall short in supporting emerging front-facing mobile AR applications, e.g., virtual try-on where a user leverages a front-facing camera to explore the effect of various products (e.g., glasses or hats) of different styles. This lack of support can be attributed to the unique challenges of obtaining 360◦HDR environment maps, an ideal format of lighting representation, from the front-facing camera and existing techniques. In this paper, we propose to leverage dual-camera streaming to generate a high-quality environment map by combining multi-view lighting reconstruction and parametric directional lighting estimation. Our preliminary results show improved rendering quality using a dual-camera setup for front-facing AR compared to a commercial solution.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes leveraging dual-camera streaming to generate high-quality environment maps by combining multi-view lighting reconstruction and parametric directional lighting estimation for front-facing mobile AR applications.
core_content.key_methodology: The method involves using LDR multi-camera video streaming as input, proposing a simple face-tracking component for stitching camera views, and extending the parametric directional lighting estimation model with back-facing camera streaming to improve completeness and color tone of the environment map and support shadow casting.
outcomes: Improved rendering quality using dual-camera setup for front-facing AR compared to commercial solutions
Characterization of challenges in supporting photorealistic front-facing mobile AR
core_content.full_text_markdown: Lighting understanding is crucial for virtual object composition, especially in mobile augmented reality (AR) applications. The common approach involves using a back-facing camera to capture the physical world and overlay virtual objects. However, this method struggles with emerging front-facing mobile AR applications like virtual try-on, where users explore products through their front-facing cameras. This paper addresses these challenges by proposing a dual-camera setup that combines multi-view lighting reconstruction and parametric directional lighting estimation to generate high-quality environment maps. The authors demonstrate improved rendering quality using this approach compared to commercial solutions.
